topic: Religion & Ethics: Significant Concepts in Issues / Debates
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does 'dominion' in Genesis necessarily mean exploitation?
items.correct: No - many theologians read it as responsible rule in God's image, paired with Genesis 2:15's command to 'till and keep' the garden; exploitation is one contested interpretation.
items.misconception: The Bible simply commands humans to use up the natural world.
items.random_wrong: Dominion refers to a board game mentioned in Genesis.
items.why: The dominion/stewardship debate is the heart of this topic; treating one reading as the obvious meaning skips the argument.

items.q: Is concern for the environment a purely modern, secular invention?
items.correct: No - religious traditions contain long-standing resources, from sabbath rest for the land to Francis of Assisi, and modern statements such as the papal encyclical Laudato Si' (2015).
items.misconception: Religions had nothing to say about the environment until secular movements forced them.
items.random_wrong: Environmental ethics was first proposed in the year 2000.
items.why: Students assume green ethics is post-religious; the syllabus expects knowledge of religious environmental teaching.

items.q: Does equality mean treating everyone in exactly the same way?
items.correct: Not necessarily - debates distinguish equal worth and rights from identical treatment; justice may require accommodating differences, such as disability access.
items.misconception: If people are equal, any difference in treatment is automatically unjust.
items.random_wrong: Equality means everyone must hold the same beliefs.
items.why: The worth/treatment distinction unlocks most equality debates in the paper, from gender roles to positive action.

items.q: What does the concept of stewardship teach?
items.correct: Humans should care for creation on God's behalf and are accountable for it
items.misconception: Humans may exploit nature without limit
items.random_wrong: Nature is divine and must never be touched
items.why: It's Humans should care for creation on God's behalf and are accountable for it. “Humans may exploit nature without limit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did Lynn White famously argue in 1967?
items.correct: That the Christian idea of dominion contributed to the ecological crisis
items.random_wrong: That the environment cannot be damaged by humans
items.why: Correct answer: That the Christian idea of dominion contributed to the ecological crisis. “That religion invented environmentalism” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Nature has intrinsic value independent of its usefulness to humans
items.misconception: Nature matters only as a resource for people
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nature has intrinsic value independent of its usefulness to humans. “Nature matters only as a resource for people” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How does the idea of 'imago Dei' support equality?
items.correct: All humans made in God's image share equal dignity and worth
items.random_wrong: Images of God must be displayed in public
items.why: The answer examiners want is: All humans made in God's image share equal dignity and worth. “Only religious believers have rights” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
